title: Lemon balm infusion for mild anxiety
subtitle: A gentle nervine for a busy, anxious mind
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried lemon balm leaves, taken for mild anxiety, nervous tension, or the restless state that comes with an overactive mind. One of the most pleasant-tasting nervine herbs in the western tradition, with a gentle lemon-and-honey flavour.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), melissa entry, nervine, carminative, and mild sedative action. Paracelsus and early European apothecary tradition, melissa as a herb for the heart and nervous system. EMA HMPC monograph on Melissa officinalis folium (2013), confirms traditional use for mild symptoms of mental stress and difficulty falling asleep.

content.text: A cup of lemon balm tea for the kind of anxiety that keeps the mind busy, circling thoughts, a restless alertness that won't settle, the tension that builds through a stressful afternoon. Lemon balm is the herbal tradition's most approachable

. It is pleasant-tasting, mild enough to drink daily, and recorded in use from the medieval apothecary through to modern monographs. The modern reading confirms traditional use for mild mental stress and trouble falling asleep. The preparation is a simple covered infusion. The lid is important for lemon balm: the scented oils that carry the calming action are the first thing to escape from an uncovered cup. See the sources note for the modern reference.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Thyroid medication: lemon balm has long been noted to have a mild effect on the thyroid. If you take thyroxine or other thyroid medicine, discuss regular lemon balm use with your prescriber. The interaction is in the records but is unlikely to matter at one or two cups a day.

Pregnancy: lemon balm infusion at normal tea strength is widely seen as safe. As with all medicinal herbs in pregnancy, regular therapeutic doses are best discussed with a midwife.

Driving: lemon balm has a mild sedative effect at higher doses. If you notice any drowsiness after drinking the tea, do not drive or operate machinery until you know how it affects you.

Seek medical care if anxiety is affecting your daily life, comes with panic attacks, brings chest pain or a fluttering heart, or carries on without a clear cause.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Dried lemon balm leaves
items.prepNote: about 1 heaped tablespoon of loose dried leaves

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: optional, lemon balm has a natural lemony sweetness

content.text: Place {{dried-lemon-balm-leaves}} of dried lemon balm leaves in the teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over and put the lid on immediately. Steep for exactly 10 minutes, no longer. Beyond 10 minutes the pleasant lemon flavour tips towards a more bitter, hay-like note, and the volatile oils that carry the nervine action are largely exhausted. Strain and drink slowly.

One cup during or after a period of stress or anxiety, or one cup an hour before bed if sleep is disrupted. Up to three cups a day during an acute anxious period for adults. Lemon balm at normal tea strength is safe for daily use; there are no significant safety concerns with long-term use at culinary-to-medicinal doses. For ongoing anxiety that is affecting daily life, the cup of lemon balm is a supportive measure, see your doctor.

Dried lemon balm loses volatile oils faster than most herbs. Buy in small quantities, keep in a sealed tin or dark glass jar, and check the smell regularly, good lemon balm should smell strongly of lemon when the tin is opened. If the smell is flat or hay-like, the stock needs replacing. Brew each cup fresh.
When NOT to use this remedy

Seek medical care for anxiety that is interfering with daily life, brings panic attacks, chest pain, or a fluttering heart, or carries on without a clear cause. Discuss with your prescriber if you take thyroid medication. The lemon balm cup is for mild, short-term anxiety, not for managing an anxiety disorder.
